claim: A novel Indium Gallium Nitride (InGaN) film has been used to form a mid-wave infrared (MWIR) sensor.

explanation.text: MWIR spans wavelengths of 3-5um which corresponds to 0.25-0.41 eV.  InN and GaN have bandgaps of 0.7 and 3.4eV, respectively, which are both too high to be active in this regime. Any compositions between these endpoints would have a bandgap between the endpoint values.

author: apl

claim: A novel 2-dimensional Indium Gallium Nitride (InGaN) film has been used to form a mid-wave infrared (MWIR) sensor.

explanation.text: MWIR spans wavelengths of 3-5um which corresponds to 0.25-0.41 eV.  InN and GaN have bandgaps of 0.7 and 3.4eV, respectively, which are both too high to be active in this regime. Reports of 2D InN and GaN suggest bandgaps in the range of 2 to 5eV, making them larger and less suitable than bulk values. Any compositions between these endpoints would have a bandgap between the endpoint values.

author: apl
comments: specifying 2-dimensional layers will impact the expected bandgap of the end-points

claim: A novel Indium Gallium Nitride (InGaN) film has been used to form a near-infrared (NIR) sensor.

explanation.text: NIR spans wavelengths of 0.7-1.1um which corresponds to 1.1-1.7 eV.  InN and GaN have bandgaps of 0.7 and 3.4eV, respectively, therefore a high In-content InGaN film would have a bandgap within this range. Any compositions between these endpoints would have a bandgap between the endpoint values.

author: apl
comments: Shifting to NIR makes the claim feasible if the InGaN is very In-rich.

claim: The addition of a superconducting coil can help recover magnetic energy otherwise lost in a normal conducting railgun.

explanation.text: noted to be feasible in the literature

author: apl
